source.title: Key Lessons for Preventing Inadvertent Mixing During Chemical Unloading Operations – Chemical Reaction and Release in Atchison, Kansas (MGPI Processing, Inc.)

event.top_event: Inadvertent mixing of incompatible chemicals (sulfuric acid into sodium hypochlorite tank)
event.incident_type: Chemical reaction and toxic gas release
actions_taken: Atchison County Department of Emergency Management ordered 11,000 citizens to shelter-in-place
Evacuation orders issued to schools and residents north of the plant
Atchison Fire Department arrived on scene at 8:05 AM and blocked nearby intersections
Emergency responders closed the discharge valve on the Harcros cargo tank
Water spray and manual activation of Mod B deluge (sprinkler) system deployed to suppress the plume
Kansas Highway Patrol monitored plume from helicopter
EMS transported injured personnel to hospital
EPA conducted on-site and perimeter air monitoring from 1:00 PM
MGPI hosted post-incident training with local emergency responders
MGPI made immediate post-incident engineering changes including dedicated locks, new pipe markers, color-coded tags, and updated procedures
Harcros and MGPI installed uniquely shaped and colored couplings on sulfuric acid fill line
MGPI installed engineering system interlocks, additional monitoring and emergency shutdown devices
MGPI modified control room air handling equipment and emergency respirator storage
event.summary: On October 21, 2016, a Harcros Chemicals cargo tank motor vehicle (CTMV) driver delivering 30% sulfuric acid to MGPI Processing, Inc. in Atchison, Kansas inadvertently connected the sulfuric acid discharge hose to the sodium hypochlorite fill line instead of the sulfuric acid fill line. Approximately 4,000 gallons of sulfuric acid mixed with 5,850 gallons of sodium hypochlorite, producing a greenish-yellow toxic cloud containing chlorine gas and other compounds. The cloud impacted workers onsite and migrated offsite. Over 140 individuals sought medical attention and six were hospitalised. Thousands of community members were ordered to shelter-in-place or evacuate. Contributing factors included: fill lines for incompatible chemicals located only 18 inches apart; identical fill line connection sizes; the sodium hypochlorite dust cap missing a split ring and therefore unsecured; inadequate pipe marking at connection points; unloading procedures not aligned with actual work practices; inadequate training for both MGPI operators and the Harcros driver; the operator leaving the unloading area before observing the connection; the driver not continuously monitoring the transfer; no automated shutdown capability for the chemical unloading area; control room ventilation drawing in the toxic cloud; and emergency escape respirators not immediately accessible to operators.
recommendations: 2017-01-I-KS-R1: MGPI to commission independent engineering evaluation of Mod B building and ventilation system and implement design changes to protect occupants from chemical release.

2017-01-I-KS-R2: MGPI to evaluate Mod B chemical transfer equipment and install engineering safeguards (alarms, interlocks, automated shutdown) to prevent and mitigate unintended reactions during bulk unloading.
2017-01-I-KS-R3: Harcros to establish refresher training program on CTMV emergency shut-off devices including drills simulating activation in incident scenarios.
2017-01-I-KS-R4: Harcros to establish a process to evaluate respiratory hazards at customer sites and provide appropriate emergency escape respirators and training to drivers.
2017-01-I-KS-R5: Atchison County LEPC/ACDEM to coordinate hazmat planning and training, review facility Risk Management Plans, conduct full-scale hazmat exercises, and apply for HMEP grant funding.
2003-13-I-LA-R22 (reiterated): ASHRAE to develop guidance on effective design and maintenance of HVAC systems to protect employees in the event of a hazardous materials release.
key_phrases: procedures not aligned with work practices

hazards.name: Incompatible bulk chemicals (sulfuric acid and sodium hypochlorite) stored and transferred in close proximity
Large quantities of hazardous chemicals (sulfuric acid, sodium hypochlorite) handled during CTMV unloading operations

threats.name: Incorrect hose connection by CTMV driver to wrong (sodium hypochlorite) fill line
Sodium hypochlorite fill line dust cap unsecured due to missing split ring
Operator left unloading area before observing driver make connection (procedure deviation)
Driver did not verify correct connection or continuously monitor transfer (procedure deviation)
Inadequate pipe marking and fill line identification at connection points
Identical size and appearance of fill line connections for incompatible chemicals
Fill lines for incompatible chemicals located only ~18 inches apart

consequences.name: Toxic chlorine gas cloud released onsite injuring workers (4 MGPI employees and Harcros driver hospitalised or treated)
Offsite toxic cloud exposure – over 140 community members sought medical attention, 5 citizens hospitalised
Mass community shelter-in-place and evacuation of ~11,000 Atchison residents
Operators forced to evacuate control room without respiratory protection due to toxic gas ingress through ventilation
Loss of process control – operators unable to shut down transfer or processes due to evacuation

controls.name: Cam lever dust caps with padlocks on fill lines (physical access prevention)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent unauthorised or incorrect access to fill lines by physically securing connection points when not in use
controls.lod_basis: Primary physical barrier preventing driver from connecting to wrong fill line

supporting_text: the sodium hypochlorite dust cap was unable to be locked because it was missing a split ring on one lever
MGPI had no design or engineering controls preventing the driver from making an incorrect connection
when executed properly, it was the only physical barrier preventing drivers from incorrectly connecting to the wrong fill line

controls.name: Operator escort and fill line identification for CTMV driver
controls.barrier_role: Operator physically shows driver the correct fill line and unlocks only the designated fill line dust cap before returning to control room
controls.lod_basis: Administrative procedural control relying on operator-driver communication and correct identification

supporting_text: The operator reports that he pointed out the location of the sulfuric acid fill line to the driver and that the driver acknowledged the location; the driver, however, reports that the operator did not point out the fill line.
The operator then returned to the Mod B building at approximately 7:47 AM before he saw the driver connect the discharge hose to the fill line.

controls.name: MGPI chemical unloading procedure – operator verification of correct connection
controls.barrier_role: Procedure requires operator to verify driver has connected hose to correct fill line and not allow connection to any other line
controls.lod_basis: Written procedural requirement for verification of correct connection before transfer begins

supporting_text: the procedure states that operators must verify the connection: they are supposed to 'have DOT-approved driver hook unloading hose to tank unloading station' and 'not allow driver to connect hose to any other connection.'
This work practice did not align with the Mod B sulfuric acid unloading procedure in two critical ways.
The CSB found that some operators were unfamiliar with these two steps in the unloading procedure.

controls.name: Harcros CTMV driver unloading procedure – verify correct vessel and continuously monitor transfer
controls.barrier_role: Harcros procedure requires driver to verify material will go into correct vessel and continuously monitor the transfer
controls.lod_basis: Written procedural requirement for driver verification and attendance during unloading per DOT 49 C.F.R. § 177.834(i)

supporting_text: the procedure states that drivers must 'carefully check to make certain that material to be transferred will be going into the correct vessel.'
the procedure states that drivers must 'continuously monitor transfer.'
the truck driver did not check to ensure the material in the truck would go into the correct vessel
the truck driver did not continuously monitor the transfer, as he was in the cab of the truck facing away from the unloading area

controls.name: Pipe marking and fill line identification at connection points
controls.barrier_role: Pipe markers and labels at fill line connection points enable drivers to identify the correct fill line before making a connection
controls.lod_basis: Passive identification control – relies on accurate, legible, and correctly placed pipe markers

supporting_text: of the five fill lines in the area, only propylene oxide had a pipe marker at its connection point
the sodium hypochlorite pipe marker appearing upside down
the sulfuric acid fill line was found to be incorrectly identified as 'hydrochloric acid' from a prior service
MGPI did not place pipe markers as close to the fill line connection points as possible

controls.name: Physical separation / distance between incompatible fill lines
controls.barrier_role: Spatial separation of fill lines for incompatible chemicals reduces likelihood of incorrect connection
controls.lod_basis: Passive engineering control – physical layout of unloading area

supporting_text: the sodium hypochlorite fill line was about 18 inches from the sulfuric acid fill line
The CSB found that the proximity of the sulfuric acid fill line to the sodium hypochlorite fill line at Mod B increased the likelihood for an incorrect connection during chemical unloading.

controls.name: Unique hose couplings and fill line connections (incompatible fittings)
controls.barrier_role: Uniquely shaped or sized hose couplings and fill line receivers prevent incorrect connections by making it physically impossible to connect the wrong hose
controls.lod_basis: Passive engineering control – not installed at time of incident; identified as absent

supporting_text: MGPI had no design or engineering controls preventing the driver from making an incorrect connection. At the time of the incident, the Mod B fill lines were similar in appearance and identically sized.
Since the incident, Harcros has worked with MGPI to select uniquely shaped transfer equipment for sulfuric acid to make it impossible for drivers to connect another delivery hose to that line

controls.name: MGPI operator and Harcros driver training on unloading procedures
controls.barrier_role: Training ensures operators and drivers understand and correctly execute critical unloading procedure steps including connection verification and continuous monitoring
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control – competency assurance for safe execution of unloading tasks

supporting_text: The CSB found these gaps to be indicative of the inadequacy of MGPI's training program.
The CSB also examined Harcros' training program and determined that it was deficient in effectively communicating the importance of the critical safety steps

some [operators] stated that the procedure did not call for verification of the correct fill line and some were unaware that the procedure required operators, not truck drivers, to open the fill line valve

controls.name: Active monitoring of unloading operations by Mod B supervisor
controls.barrier_role: Supervisory active monitoring identifies deviations from unloading procedures and ensures corrective action
controls.lod_basis: Management oversight control to detect and correct procedural non-conformance

supporting_text: the CSB found that MGPI's active monitoring process did not identify that operators were not performing procedures as written
the CSB found no records or accounts of operator deviations
The Mod B supervisor was off duty on the day of the incident

controls.name: Sodium hypochlorite bulk tank level indicator and high-level alarm
controls.barrier_role: Level indicator alarms in control system when tank level approaches capacity, alerting operators to abnormal conditions during transfer
controls.lod_basis: Active instrumented safeguard – detection and alarm only; no automatic shutdown configured

supporting_text: MGPI has a sodium hypochlorite tank level indicator that signals an alarm in the control system when the level approaches tank capacity; however, it only notifies operators of the high level and does not automatically shut down the transfer
At 7:59 AM on the day of the incident, the level indicator in the sodium hypochlorite bulk tank triggered an alarm after the sulfuric acid began flowing into the tank.

controls.name: Automated process interlock / automatic transfer shutdown (not installed at time of incident)
controls.barrier_role: Automated interlock on pressure, temperature, or level would signal transfer valve to close automatically without operator action in event of process deviation
controls.lod_basis: Active engineered safeguard – absent at time of incident; identified as critical gap

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, MGPI did not have instrumentation in the Mod B process control system that would have automatically shut down the transfer of chemicals in the event of a process deviation
an interlock on a pressure or level indicator would have signaled the transfer valve to automatically close, without requiring action from operators
Activating the emergency stop buttons would have halted the processes, but would not have mitigated the reaction, as there was no remote shutdown capability for the chemical unloading area.

controls.name: CTMV pneumatic emergency shutoff switch (front of trailer)
controls.barrier_role: Pneumatic emergency shutoff at front of cargo tank stops flow of product from trailer in emergency
controls.lod_basis: Active engineered safeguard on CTMV – present but not activated by driver

supporting_text: the tanker training log indicated that the driver was aware of the location of a pneumatic emergency shutoff switch for the internal valve that would stop the flow of product, at the front of the trailer; however, the CSB found that the driver did not trigger the emergency shutoff switch, despite being in the cab at the time of the incident

controls.name: Mod B building positive pressure control room and HVAC system
controls.barrier_role: Positive pressure control room designed to prevent harmful gases from adjacent indoor process area entering the control room and protect operators
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control for occupant protection – designed only for internal process area gases, not external releases

supporting_text: The Mod B building has a positive pressure control room, designed only to prevent harmful gases from the adjacent indoor process area from entering the control room.
design considerations did not include protecting occupants from harmful gases or vapors from outside the building
One of these intakes is located on the first level of the structure, adjacent to the tank farm and bulk chemical transfer area.
the cloud had entered through the Mod B building intake before operators became aware of the release

controls.name: Emergency escape respirators for Mod B operators
controls.barrier_role: Emergency escape respirators (face pieces and escape bottles) enable operators to safely evacuate in the event of a toxic gas release
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defence for personnel protection during emergency evacuation

supporting_text: the CSB found that Mod B operators did not have immediate access to escape respirators for the evacuation
the respirator face pieces were not stored in a readily accessible location or had been moved
all three operators were forced to evacuate the building without respiratory protection
The operators' practice was to lock respirator face pieces in their lockers at the end of each shift

controls.name: Mod B deluge (sprinkler) system for chemical release suppression
controls.barrier_role: Water deluge system suppresses toxic gas cloud produced by chemical reaction in unloading area
controls.lod_basis: Mitigation system activated by emergency responders to suppress the chlorine plume

supporting_text: MGPI had a deluge system to mitigate propylene oxide releases in the unloading area, which emergency responders were able to manually activate to help suppress the cloud produced by the reaction.
Deluge systems were on at this time to mitigate the release.

controls.name: Emergency response – shelter-in-place, evacuation, and hazmat response
controls.barrier_role: Community shelter-in-place and evacuation orders, fire department and hazmat response to limit public exposure and close discharge valve
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response and consequence mitigation after release

supporting_text: ACDEM advised 11,000 Atchison citizens to shelter-in-place
Responders were able to safely close the discharge valve on the cargo tank and turn off the truck engine.
The Atchison Fire Department (AFD) received notification of the chemical release at 8:02 AM and units arrived on scene at 8:05 AM.
By approximately 10:30 AM, the Kansas Highway Patrol reported to emergency responders that the plume was on the north and west outskirts of the city and dissipating rapidly.

controls.name: Personal protective equipment (PPE) for CTMV driver during unloading
controls.barrier_role: Chemical resistant PPE (coat, gloves, hard hat, face shield, safety glasses) provides driver with protection during unloading operations
controls.lod_basis: PPE as last line of defence for driver during chemical handling

supporting_text: The driver donned a chemical resistant coat, rubber gloves, a hard hat with a face shield, and safety glasses.
Harcros does not require delivery drivers to wear respirators unless its customers require them.

the CSB found that Harcros' tanker training log states that all proper PPE must be donned according to the SDS for the chemical being delivered. The CSB determined that this type of respirator was not provided to the driver on the day of the incident.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) for Mod B unloading operations
controls.barrier_role: Voluntary PHA identifies potential hazards and safeguards for chemical unloading including risk of wrong chemical transfer
controls.lod_basis: Management system control – hazard identification and risk assessment process

supporting_text: In the most recent PHA for Mod B before the incident from March 2015, MGPI identified the potential for the wrong chemical to be transferred into the sodium hypochlorite bulk tank due to operator error or a 'bad shipment' of a chemical. MGPI included mostly generic administrative safeguards, such as training and procedures, to prevent the transfer of a wrong chemical; most of which failed to prevent or mitigate the October 2016 incident

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
